speaker: interviewer
text: Just look at it. Just half. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not on the girls, because it makes them less--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. This is tape 3.

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is October 31, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Eva Schutz. And the location is London, England.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian helped us to move into this new accommodation, and took even Jan in a car, because Jan had a knee injury. Well, my mother lost her job because it was a, a oil company, and the Russians didn't want anybody-- a Pole or Jew, whatever, in a oil company.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Dr. Kreisberg lost his company. But he was a very rich man, and he didn't mind that. We were all the time in contact with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jan went to school. George went to school. The school was very near. They didn't learn much there, I suppose. They had to learn Ukraine language and, naturally, all about the Russian revolution and all what was all the Russian. We are the, the good people. And the Poles were the terrible, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] terrible people. Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got a job then in a hospital in the kitchen for-- the milk kitchen for children. And there-- where my education or my learning of diet- of diets came in very handy. Not that we had milk and all the things I was told in Vienna to give babies, because we didn't. But we managed. And life went on for awhile, more or less. We had no money because the banks were closed. We, I think, could change 300 zlotys to 300 rubles. 300 rubles was nothing. And no-- the shops were completely empty. In one night, the shops were completely empty. There was nothing in shops. You couldn't get anything, where the things-- whether the Russians grabbed it or the owners hid it. But I don't know what they did with it. But there was nothing. But the peasants still came to Lwow to sell, so to say, their goods. But you can't-- couldn't pay them with money. But you had to give them things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, for a liter of milk and six eggs and some cream, you had to give them two chairs or a dress or whatever this woman who came into your flat wanted. She choose. And if you wanted to have something to eat, you had to get it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were also rations card-- ration cards. But we-- there were queues, and you didn't get anything. So slowly, slowly, my mother gave nearly everything in the flat for-- just for food. I got sometimes something in, in the hospital, which I brought home. My mother managed wonderful. And she also took Jan's education in her hand. And whatever Jan knows now about Polish history, about the-- the even the beginning of mathematics, and so on, he-- my mother taught him that. She spoke to him about books, about poems. She knew everything by heart. So Jan-- even if they didn't have books there, only a few-- my mother took the most important books with her into those two rooms. She knew so much by heart that Jan learned it. And he knows today poems which he never read.

speaker: interviewee
text: So life went on like that. I-- we were in touch with the family. Professor [NON-ENGLISH] was quite well off because he was, naturally, an international, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] celebrated professor. And the Russians knew what was good. My [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brother-in-law, Milek, also was very much liked by the Russians because he was a good doctor, and so on. So the Russian left it-- the doctors alone. But one night, all the wives of the doctors who were in the army, who went with the army, were taken and deported to Russia. They missed me out because they went to the address where we lived when Max was in Lwow, in the house of his mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they asked about the wife of Maximilian-- Dr. Maximilian Schutz, who is a captain in the Polish Army, she said she has no idea where she is. She was in Warsaw. And she doesn't know. The Russian was-- they never looked long enough for somebody. But when I came next day to the hospital, they looked at me like a ghost that I am there because all the other doctors' wives were just-- were deported to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came home and told my mother that. And she said, well, you see, it is not as bad as that. Don't be so pessimistic. Don't think of Hitler and Stalin. Just think of bringing the child up. And we will survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't think there is much to say about-- more about the Russian--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: --occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: --happened next, after the Russian occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Russian occupation, there was-- well, one day, the German moved in. There was war with Russia. It was the-- we didn't know anything about that. So they just came with their tanks. And that was the end of the good time, which I found not good enough. But, now, really, the hell started. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first thing was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wearing the Star of David. Everybody have to wear the Star of David. It was death sentence if a Jew was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] found without it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I still went to this hospital because it was-- this hospital still existed. Every day, my-- every day, you heard and saw the most-- the most terrible things which you can imagine. You can't imagine what, what I saw while walking to, to, to my work because Jews were not allowed to use the tram. And Jews were not allowed to walk on the sidewalk. We had to walk on the street. Once I was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] beaten by a German or Ukrainian soldier because I was-- I wasn't thinking. I just walked on the sidewalk-- and 'Jude, Jude.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, he, he-- with his rifle, gave me a good few over my head. And somebody found me and brought me back home. I was-- then-- but I was alive. I wasn't dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, there was-- there was real hunger and especially fear. I never know whether I will find my mother and Jan at home when I go home. I left at 5 o'clock in the morning and came back late afternoon or perhaps in the evening. In the meantime, sometimes I couldn't even go to the hospital because I was caught by Germans who, who wanted the streets cleaned, the snow cleaned. The-- it was bitter cold. And I have no, no warm clothes because all-- well, the furs were confiscated at once. Jews had to give the furs at once. But I have to go back now, when I talk about furs. The Judenrat was naturally at once installed. And the Gestapo was asking the Judenrat to give them a thousand fur coats or a thousand [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] watches or a thousand-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what. And then they started-- a thousand people had to be brought to a certain place for hard work. And those people disappeared then. And then they wanted this and they wanted that. And one day, when the Judenrat could not [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] give them what they asked-- I can't remember what it was. Perhaps I never knew. They just killed the three most important of the Judenrat and hanged them on the Platz [NON-ENGLISH], the middle of Lwow, on a balcony. And they were hanging there for a few days. Amongst them was my uncle, a lawyer, Josef Parnas. Then one day I came back from the hospital. And there was my mother and Jan sitting on the stairs-- my mother with two suitcases or boxes or whatever it was, Jan with the, the cage with the canary-- waiting for me. They were thrown out of there-- all Jews out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they waited for me, even not knowing whether I will come back. I could have been caught in the meantime somewhere on the street and not come back. So we went to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Kreisberg was in the ghetto already. As I told before, he was a wealthy man. He, I think, that-- what I was told later-- he gave to the Judenrat a lot of not only money, but gold, jewelry, in case they will be caught. And so they can bribe the Gestapo or whoever caught them, or if he needs some money. Because naturally, he couldn't have gold with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Judenrat always was able to provide him whatever he needed. So he got two rooms in the ghetto. And he got a little room for my mother and Jan and me there. So I know where we were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was with a Jewish family who were not very pleased to see us in this room. Because before we went-- also, they were paid all the time for the room to keep it free. They used it. And when we arrived, they couldn't use it anymore. And they were a big family. And they had to squeeze themselves more. But we three in a very small room. There was one bed, had two mattresses, and the-- it-- again, that was arranged by Dr. Kreisberg. So one mattress was Jan's and I on the floor, and one my-- on the bed, my mother slept. I slept with Jan on the floor. And the, the hospital was transferred to a school building in the ghetto. That was not a real hospital. It was a school building. But they did everything to make it-- well, the beds and whatever the Jews needed, which was not much for Germans. Jews needed anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started going there. It was very near, this hospital. My mother was with Jan in this little room. Again, the education of Jan flourished there. There was always something to write, always something to read.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jan was then 10 years. He read in one go. It, it was incredible what my mother did to him-- that mean for him, not to him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, life was just one hell. I can't tell anything about it. More people disappeared. Naturally, gossip, true and not true-- every day something worse. I lost touch with Max family. I knew that my mother-in-law had false papers and went into the country. She looked very good. She didn't look Jewish. George was sent to the country to a friend of Milek and stayed the whole war there with a boy of his age, the son of the owner of the-- this estate, who was, as a Pole, also switched to another estate because the Germans liked it. They didn't like people to stay in one place. So he went with this, this family-- this family. He was apparently the nephew of this owner. I knew that I had already-- that Lusia prepared-- not her-- not she, but somebody who prepared false papers-- prepared for me and for Jan and my mother, papers. And I knew where I can get them if I need them. But the time was not ripe for that. I was with my mother in the ghetto. My mother refused to have any false papers. My mother, who was never religious, I think never went to synagogue, just refused. I was born Jewish, and I will die as a Jewess. That was her saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] times-- everyday was worse. I saw it in the hospital. The Germans came to the hospital. They took babies, throw them out of the window. They killed people in the bed, outside the bed, whatever. Whatever they wanted to do, they did. Nobody could do anything about it. So I started taking-- my mother, I think, begged me to take Jan with me. She can't look after him anymore. And if something happens to her, what will happen to Jan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't tell her if something happens to her, it will happen to Jan also. If something happens to me, it will happen to Jan also. So I took him every day. And one day came where they didn't want to let me through, the German soldiers which were outside the hospital. I told them I have to get through, that the child is ill. And it has to go to the hospital. And we went in. And Jan went to the library, where they usually played with other doctor's children, because all the doctors and nurses brought their children there. And I went to the kitchen to do whatever I had to do. There was a lot of shouting going on. The Germans went through the hospital like fire. And suddenly, somebody told me all the children were, were taken from the library. Your, your Jan is taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I left whatever I did. And I went down into the hall where the Gestapo was standing and the two doctors from the hospital were standing. Everybody shaking, everybody, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw Jan being put on the truck. And I took my overall off and thought, now I tell them what I think of them. And I told him, the Gestapo who was standing there with his rifle or machine-- whatever they called it. And I told him that [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I told him what I was thinking of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us that in English what you just said in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] are pigs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rest of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That this is my son, and if you take him from me, I know that's the end of me also. So I better tell you what I think of you. And I told him. I told him. The doctors were shaking like leaves because they thought now the German will take the, the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what we call it-- the automatic rifles there and will just go through everybody. The Gestapo stared at me. And I stared at him. And he told to this militiaman-- there, there was a Jewish militia who was told to do all the dirty jobs-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Give the woman the child back. And they took Jan off the truck and brought him back to me. Well, how I felt you don't know. And I think it's very difficult for me today to, to know how I felt. I just felt-- well, I didn't say, thank you very much. I just took Jan and went up to the kitchen. But then I decided whether my mother wants to go or not, I'm going. We went back home. And when I came home, there was nobody in the whole house. Everybody was taken, the whole family where we were staying with and my mother. Everything was opened. There just was-- so I packed whatever I could in more or less good order because I didn't want to, to be looked at a kind of refugee or something. I tidied myself up and dressed Jan as, as well as I could in a few, few things, one on the other, and went to the-- just took the Magen David off, left it there-- and Jan's-- and went into town.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left the ghetto, went into town, looking very proud, not fearing any Germans, and went to this place where I was told my papers will be. I stayed overnight. There were-- they were Poles who helped-- who helped the underground army. They told me where to go for the night, because I told them I'm going to Warsaw. They didn't believe me that I'm going to Warsaw. But I-- next day, after we got something to eat, I walked to the-- I walked to the station. I spoke to some German soldiers in German, just to show that I'm not Polish-- the Polish-- because there were lots of Poles and Jews, I'm sorry to say, who looked out for Jews. They were employed by the Gestapo, not that it helped them to survive. They were killed very soon. So-- but I was very conscious of that. And that's why people went from one town to the other. They were not-- I was not known in, in Warsaw. But I was not well-known, but I was known in, in Lwow. And even I was afraid of Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I spoke to some German soldiers so that just the Poles would think that I am German. So perhaps they hated me because they hated German, naturally. So did I. And boarded the train-- I told Jan not to open his mouth because he didn't speak German-- and went to Warsaw. Can I ask you what effect was all this having on Jan, a young 11-year-old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We-- I had no time to, to look on effects. Later, I was told, how could you do this? Or how could you do that? And how did the child react? No time to look for emotions or things like that. We had to-- either we had to get on or we had to get off. And I didn't want to get off, especially after what they-- well, what, what I saw and what they did to my mother. I heard later through Dr. Kreisberg-- Dr. Kreisberg was taken on this-- to this train. But he was bribed off. That means he was-- the German were bribed to be let off. And he let me know later that my mother was on this train, and the train was apparently going to Treblinka. My mother had poison with her. And I hope she took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the poison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cyankali. We all had it. Well, I didn't take it because I had the child to look after. So there we went to Warsaw. And nothing happens in the train. Naturally everybody's gossiped about something, about the German. There were Poles. There were Germans in the-- in the [BACKROUND NOISE] compartment. And when-- I knew only one family was-- which was not Jewish in Warsaw. And I thought I will go to them. And naturally, I didn't dare to go to anybody of my family. I didn't know whether they are alive, whether they are in the Warsaw Ghetto, whether they-- they didn't want to see me, certainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know this family you were going to?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was friends of, of Lusia and Milek. That was a theater director-- doctor-- a director, [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. A very, very famous name, [NON-ENGLISH]. I will tell you later how it is spelled. They were-- no, on the way to them, I gave the address to the-- to the cab driver. And he went on with the horse. And I saw somebody running after us. And I told him to stop because somebody wants to talk to him. He stopped. And this man came up to me and told me, you have a Jewish child with you. And I said, that is not a Jewish child. It is my son. And he didn't believe me. And he started-- if I gave him a thousand zloty, he will forget about it that I have a Jewish child with me. And I say, that is my son. It is not a Jewish child. I didn't look Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Jan doing while this was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He was sitting there and just listening, I suppose. And naturally-- he spoke Polish, naturally. He didn't speak German. He spoke Polish. And so I told the cab driver, now take us. This man says things I can't believe in. And take us to the next police station. And there I will explain what I am and that that is my son. I have papers. I had papers. My paper and his paper. So he left me. And I came to [NON-ENGLISH]. And when they opened the door and saw me, they had, really, a fit. But I told them I won't stay. I will get in touch with a cousin of mine which was married to a gentile. And I'm sure she will help me because she was very fond of my mother and a bit of me also and of Jan also. She had no children. She always wanted a child. So Jan was a little bit special.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she-- I went the next day to her. Again, she looked at me like coming from the other world. She thought that I am already dead. And she arranged for Jan to be taken to a-- to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] monastery, where the monks-- Marians-- in-- outside Warsaw. Very far from Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. That was quite far in-- for, for me, everything was far. And for me, she arranged to look after a friend of hers, which was not Jewish. She was ill and needed help in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was for the time being. But I needed an identity card. And again, Maryla arranged that for me, this identity card. I had-- I had a, a German-- Excuse me, who was Maryla again?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my cousin--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who was married to a gentile. But she was Jewish. And she was very brave and a bit foolish, in my opinion. But that was her life. She stayed in the same house where she stayed before the war, where everybody knew her, and everybody knew from which family she comes. But people liked her. And she was lucky. At the end of the war, she was taken away. But then, after this lady I looked after got healthy again and moved somewhere into the country, to family-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It would be a good time--
